era_context: The Abbasid Caliphate under al-Ma'mun (r. 813–833) is at its zenith, pursuing aggressive theological and scientific patronage. The Tang Dynasty in China is in decline under Emperor Xianzong, facing military pressures and provincial fragmentation. In Europe, Charlemagne's Frankish Empire has recently fragmented; Louis the Pious rules the Carolingian realm. The Byzantine Empire under Theophilus I continues iconoclastic policies. This is a period of significant intellectual ferment in the Islamic world, military consolidation in East Asia, and political instability in the West.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Sub-Saharan Africa (limited written sources; archaeological record incomplete)
Scandinavia and Northern Europe (sparse contemporary documentation)
Pre-Columbian Americas (no written records in Old World sense)

events.title: Abbasid Caliph al-Ma'mun establishes the House of Wisdom in Baghdad
events.description: Al-Ma'mun formalizes and expands the House of Wisdom (Bayt al-Hikma), an institution dedicated to translation, preservation, and advancement of Greek, Persian, Indian, and Islamic knowledge. This marks the institutional peak of the Abbasid translation movement, which had begun under al-Mansur and al-Rashid. The House becomes a center for mathematics, astronomy, medicine, and philosophy, attracting scholars from across the Islamic world and beyond. This patronage will profoundly influence European intellectual recovery centuries later.
key_figures: Hunayn ibn Ishaq (physician and translator, active slightly later)

sources.name: Historiographic consensus on Abbasid intellectual history

events.certainty_note: The House of Wisdom existed and was patronized by al-Ma'mun, but the exact date of formal establishment is debated. Some sources suggest earlier foundations under al-Rashid; the 830s represent the period of documented expansion and formalization. Dating is approximate within the reign of al-Ma'mun (813–833).
cross_references: Follows from intellectual patronage under al-Rashid (r. 786–809)
Precedes major translation of Aristotle's works into Arabic (9th century)

events.title: Tang Dynasty military campaign against Uyghur Khaganate
events.description: Emperor Xianzong of Tang authorizes military operations against the Uyghur Khaganate in Central Asia, reflecting ongoing competition for control of the Silk Road and tributary relationships. The campaign is part of broader Tang efforts to reassert control over frontier regions amid internal military fragmentation. These campaigns are costly and ultimately unsuccessful in reversing Tang decline.
key_figures: Emperor Xianzong of Tang (r. 805–820) — note: Xianzong died in 820; this may refer to campaigns under his successor Muzong or Jingzong

sources.name: Jiu Tangshu (Old History of Tang, 10th century)
Xin Tangshu (New History of Tang, 11th century)

events.certainty_note: Tang military campaigns in Central Asia during this period are well-attested, but specific dating to 830 is uncertain. The reign of Xianzong ended in 820; campaigns in 830 would fall under his successors. Dating is approximate within the broader period of Tang–Uyghur conflict (820s–840s).
cross_references: Part of broader Tang decline and loss of Central Asian influence
Precedes major Uyghur–Tang alliance shift in 840s

events.title: Byzantine Emperor Theophilus I continues iconoclastic persecution
events.description: Emperor Theophilus I (r. 829–842) intensifies the second iconoclastic period (813–843), persecuting icon veneration and monks who resist the ban on religious images. This represents a continuation of theological conflict that has divided the Byzantine Church and society for over a century. Theophilus is known for particularly harsh measures against iconodule monks, including imprisonment and torture. The policy will be reversed after his death.

sources.name: Life of Saint Theodore of Studion (hagiographic source)

events.certainty_note: Theophilus's reign and iconoclastic policies are well-documented in Byzantine sources. The year 830 falls within his documented reign (829–842), and persecution of iconodules is attested throughout this period.
cross_references: Precedes reversal of iconoclasm under Theodora (843)

events.title: Abbasid Caliph al-Ma'mun's Mihna (inquisition) against theological dissent
events.description: Al-Ma'mun initiates or intensifies the Mihna, a theological inquisition designed to enforce Mu'tazilite rationalist doctrine (particularly the doctrine of the created Qur'an) against traditionalist scholars. This represents state enforcement of theological orthodoxy and marks a significant moment of tension between caliphal authority and religious scholars. The Mihna will persist beyond al-Ma'mun's death and eventually be reversed under al-Mutawakkil (847–861).

sources.name: Historiographic consensus on Abbasid religious history
Biographies of Ahmad ibn Hanbal (later sources)

events.certainty_note: The Mihna occurred during al-Ma'mun's reign (813–833), but the exact date of initiation is debated. Some sources place it earlier (c. 815–820); others suggest intensification in the 820s–830s. Dating to 830 is approximate.
cross_references: Precedes reversal of Mihna under al-Mutawakkil (847)

disconfirming_evidence: The dating and significance of the House of Wisdom's formal establishment in 830 is contested. Some modern scholars argue that the institution was more fluid and less formally organized than later sources suggest, and that the 'House of Wisdom' as a discrete institution may be a later historiographic construct. The Mihna's relationship to al-Ma'mun's intellectual patronage is paradoxical: he simultaneously promoted rational inquiry and enforced theological orthodoxy. Modern scholarship emphasizes that these were not contradictory in the Mu'tazilite framework, which valued rational theology, but the apparent tension has led some historians to question the coherence of al-Ma'mun's policies or to revise dating of specific persecutions.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 830 CE is moderate but heavily weighted toward the Islamic world and Byzantine Empire. Abbasid sources are abundant but often compiled centuries later (Ibn al-Nadim, al-Mas'udi, etc.), introducing potential anachronism and legendary embellishment. Byzantine sources are more contemporary but reflect court ideology. Tang sources are later compilations (Jiu Tangshu, Xin Tangshu) and may conflate or misdate events. Sub-Saharan Africa, Northern Europe, and the Americas are essentially absent from contemporary written records. The surviving sources reflect elite, urban, literate cultures and likely underrepresent rural, military, and economic history.

graph_edges.note: Al-Ma'mun's intellectual patronage and theological enforcement occurred simultaneously, reflecting the Mu'tazilite synthesis of reason and faith.
Theophilus's iconoclastic policies were reversed after his death, leading to the restoration of icon veneration in 843.
Al-Ma'mun's Mihna was reversed under al-Mutawakkil, who restored traditionalist theology.
